title: Keeping a livestock health diary
subtitle: The observation log that makes patterns visible over time.
excerpt: A health diary is not the same as the statutory medicine record. It is the observation notebook where you note anything unusual: a ewe with a cough, a pig that fed slowly, a hen that did not come out in the morning. Over months it reveals patterns that a stock memory alone cannot hold and gives any vet far more useful information than 'she seemed fine until yesterday'.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for all farmed livestock categories. AHDB Beef & Lamb health planning guidance. Farming Advisory Service, record keeping for small holdings.

glossaryTerms.definition: A written agreement between a keeper and their vet setting out routine preventive treatments, monitoring protocols, and response plans for common conditions on the holding. Required for some farm assurance schemes; recommended for all livestock holdings.

content.text: The medicine record is a legal requirement. The health diary is what makes it useful. A vet diagnosing a problem needs to know when the first signs appeared, which animals were affected first, what the weather had been doing, and whether anything changed in feed or management in the weeks before.

The health diary should capture any observation that deviates from normal, however minor at the time. Examples: '14 May: ewe 7 favoured near fore, looked sound by evening'; '6 June: pig 2 fed slowly at morning feed, normal by afternoon'; '22 July: lower egg count in morning (9 not 12), checked for hidden nests, none found'. Notes need not be long. A date, the animal, and one factual sentence.

A spiral notebook on a hook inside the feed shed works. A spreadsheet works. A phone memo app works. The format matters less than the habit. Most smallholders who find they never fill in the notebook do better with a phone app note because the phone is always on their person during the morning round.

When a treatment is given, record it in the statutory medicine log and add a cross-reference note in the health diary: '4 June, ewe 7 drenched for worms (see medicine log)'. The two records together tell the full story of that animal's health; the medicine log alone does not show what prompted the decision.

At the end of each year, read back through the diary. Common patterns (coccidiosis in lambs every May, a particular ewe that goes lame repeatedly, summer respiratory problems in housed pigs) suggest management changes worth making. Bring the diary to the annual vet visit; a
built from observed patterns is far more useful than a generic checklist.
